article_title: Google Cloud Run Worker Pools Reach GA With Open-Source CREMA Autoscaler and Blackwell GPU Support
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Google Cloud Run Worker Pools Reach GA With Open-Source CREMA Autoscaler and Blackwell GPU Support
content_preview.summary: Google Cloud Run Worker Pools hit general availability on April 14, 2026, shipping with Blackwell GPU support and CREMA, an open-source KEDA-based autoscaler for queue-driven workloads.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Google Cloud made several significant announcements in the days before its Cloud Next 2026 conference opened on April 22, shipping a cluster of generally available features that together position Cloud Run as a credible platform for background compute workloads that have historically lived on Kubernetes or bare virtual machines. On April 14, Cloud Run Worker Pools reached [general availability](https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/release-notes), followed one day later by the GA o...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured 1,101-word News article covering three related GA announcements (Worker Pools, Blackwell GPU, Cloud Run MCP server) around Google Cloud Next 2026. The article correctly separates what is confirmed from what remains unknown (RTX PRO 6000 pricing for worker pools, CREMA managed-service timeline), and the analysis section draws a coherent synthesis without overstating Google's position. The 'What We Don't Know' section is a strong journalistic addition. Word count is within the News category range (400–1,200 words).
editor_notes.source_verification: All six sources fetched and verified. Key claims confirmed against primary sources: Worker Pools GA date April 14 2026 (release notes); GPU GA date April 13 2026 (release notes); Cloud Run MCP server GA April 15 2026 (release notes); '40% less for CPU and memory' claim is verbatim from exploring-cloud-run-worker-pools-and-kafka-autoscaler; CREMA 8 supported scalers (Apache Kafka, Cron, GCP Pub/Sub, GitHub Runner Scaler, Prometheus, RabbitMQ Queue, Redis Lists, Temporal) confirmed on GitHub; RTX PRO 6000 96 GB VRAM and 5-second cold start confirmed in GPU docs; 20 vCPUs / 80 GiB minimum confirmed; Chris Curro quote confirmed verbatim; Ajay Nair quote confirmed verbatim; CREMA v1.1 / KEDA v2.19 confirmed; memory leak fix before April 29 2026 confirmed. The two flagged sources (docs.cloud.google.com) are legitimate Google official documentation subdomains — cloud.google.com is already on the allowlist. The '100% message durability' phrase is not verbatim in the Estée Lauder source (source says 'no user message is lost') but is a fair editorial summary and not presented as a direct quote.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specific factual claims verified against their cited sources. Dates, technical specifications, and quotes are accurate. The article correctly attributes the cost reduction to 'CPU and memory' on worker pools (not a blanket Cloud Run discount). CREMA scaler count (8) is accurate. The memory leak detail and fix date (April 29 2026) are confirmed by the GitHub repo changelog.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, professional tone throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing. Ajay Nair's quote ('fundamentally changed how we manage our model deployments') is a marketing statement but properly attributed. No AI self-reference. The analysis section expresses editorial judgment on CREMA's operational complexity but does so factually and without embellishment.
editor_notes.originality: The article synthesizes six distinct sources into a coherent narrative covering three interrelated GA releases. The framing as a 'cluster of GA announcements' positioned ahead of Cloud Next 2026 is accurate and adds context beyond any single source. The Estée Lauder case study section draws meaningfully on the dedicated case study post.
concerns: docs.cloud.google.com domain not in source_allowlist.txt — legitimate Google documentation subdomain, should be added to allowlist. Adding it resolves the only pipeline warning.
recommendations: Add docs.cloud.google.com to config/source_allowlist.txt to resolve the allowlist warning for future submissions citing official Google documentation.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Solid, well-sourced News article. All specific claims verified against primary sources. The pipeline warning (docs.cloud.google.com not in allowlist) is a configuration gap, not a sourcing problem — the URLs are legitimate official Google documentation. Approving with correction to add docs.cloud.google.com to the source allowlist.
